text: Klippel-Troy syndrome is a rare congenital vascular disorder characterized by venous, lymphatic and capillary malformations with associated soft tissue and bone hypertrophy of the limb. Vascular malformations could affect gastrointestinal tract, liver, spleen or pelvic organs and may present with recurrent gastrointestinal bleed.

Our patient is a forty five year old male patient who was diagnosed with Klippel-Trenoy syndrome few years back. He had noticed progressive lower limb swelling and recurrent episodes of blood in stools. He required multiple blood transfusions for anaemia every two to three monthly for the past three to four years with recent aggravation in last four to five months.
The colonoscopy image shows multiple venous angiomas throughout the rectum and sigmoid colon, while the proximal large bowel appears normal.

Contrast CT scan of the patient has revealed multiple angiometer involving the spleen and as we come down the multiple malformation pelvis in the true pelvis around the mesorectum and towards the right gluteal region in the subcutaneous plane and towards right lower limb. In this reconstruction, you can see the pelvic angiometer as well as subcutaneous angiometer and right gluteal region.

The surgical team utilizes a five-port approach, including an umbilical camera and right working and retracting ports. The procedure involves navigating around a bulky sigmoid colon with multiple vascular malformations, visible angiomas involving the spleen, and angiomas affecting the liver.

The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon and identifies an avascular portion for dissection. Using ultrasonic scissors, the avascular segment is dissected and divided. A window is created, and a cotton tape is tied around the recto sigmoid region to provide traction, minimizing bleeding risk from sharp instrument handling.

The surgeon lifts the sigmoid colon and places additional retracting ports. Retraction is performed using suction cannulae to minimize bleeding risk. Dissection proceeds through the avascular mesocolon of the rectosigmoid, extending towards the pelvis.

The surgeon carefully dissects the mesorectum using ultrasonic scissors, navigating through a relatively vascular plane while avoiding angiomas on the pelvic side walls. The dissection progresses towards the pelvis, focusing on the left side, where the left pelvic peritoneum is divided. The thin patient's ureter is visible through the peritoneal reflection, and care is taken to avoid injury.
The surgeon divides the sigmoid mesentery near the mesocolic vessels, dissecting, clipping, and dividing individual sigmoid branches in preparation for resection of the involved sigmoid and rectum.

The surgeon directs the dissection towards the pelvis, aiming to reach the lower rectum below the peritoneal reflection. The posterior rectum is dissected using ultrasonic sutures, maintaining a plane with minimal vascular tissue.

The surgeon reaches close to the pelvic floor during dissection, assessing the colon length for anastomosis. The normal descending colon and splenic flexure are mobilized using a monopolar hook, with dissection occurring in relatively normal tissue planes.

The surgeon mobilizes the entire sigmoid colon, ensuring it can reach the pelvis for a tension-free anastomosis. The final dissection is performed in the lower pelvis, developing the posterior mesoacral plane down to the low pelvis. Lateral tissues are carefully divided, avoiding injury to the autonomic nerves.

The surgeon carefully dissects multiple vessels located in the lateral pelvic wall and predominantly in the anterior space between the rectum and bladder. The rectovesical peritoneum is divided, and the rectum is mobilized below the peritoneal reflection, with meticulous attention to the vessels just beneath the peritoneum.

The surgeon tapers the mesorectum using a harmonic scalpel and bipolar instrument, addressing bleeding from multiple vessels due to thickened tissues. The rectal wall appears thicker than normal, likely from recurrent inflammation. A 15mm trocar is introduced, and a black, 60mm thick cartridge is applied for rectal transection. The rectum is then divided.

Hemostasis is achieved, and the descending colon and sigmoid are visually assessed for sufficient length for an anastomosis. The proximal margin of the normal colon is marked, and the specimen is extracted through a Pfannenstiel incision. A straight colorectal anastomosis is performed under direct vision, achieving a tension-free connection.
This patient had an uneventful recovery and the blood loss during surgery was around two fifty ml and for the eight months post op he is doing well without further bleeding.
